name: Voice-based community media platforms in rural India
description: This domain examines mobile and IVR-based community media systems like Mobile Vaani and CGNet Swara that enable citizen journalism, social development, and interaction among low-income rural populations in India. Readings cover design lessons, community mobilization strategies, impact pathways, and technical innovations such as automated voice-based question-answering and voice versus keypress surveys. It also addresses inclusive engagement of remote and marginalized communities, including COVID-19 response networks and WhatsApp-based journalism tools.

background_concepts.concept: Interactive Voice Response (IVR) and DTMF signaling
background_concepts.why_needed: Nearly every reading here describes voice forums built on IVR, keypad (DTMF) navigation, and text-to-speech, but assumes the reader already understands how an automated telephony menu system captures and routes caller input.

suggested_resources.title: What Is IVR? - Interactive Voice Response Explained (AWS)
What Is Interactive Voice Response (IVR)? (IBM Think)

name: Community radio operations and automation
description: This domain focuses on the technical and operational infrastructure supporting community radio stations in India, including automation systems, content distribution architectures for rural areas, and answering machine systems like Phonepeti. Readings explore how ICTs meet the operational needs of these stations, as well as their commercialization and interactive programming such as singing competitions combining radio with IVRS.

background_concepts.concept: Community radio licensing and regulation in India (GOPA, CRS policy)
background_concepts.why_needed: These readings discuss running community radio stations in India within a specific institutional and legal framework (non-profit eligibility, content quotas, advertising limits, the Grant of Permission Agreement) that they reference without explaining.

suggested_resources.title: Policy Guidelines for Setting up Community Radio Stations in India (MIB / Broadcast Seva)
How to Start a Community Radio Station (CRS): Step by Step Guide

name: Computational detection and analysis of media bias and political polarization
description: This domain covers quantitative and computational approaches to measuring media bias, political polarization, and information diffusion across blogs, Twitter, and news coverage, in both US and Indian contexts. Readings range from crowdsourced and aspect-level bias analysis to studies of polarization, structural virality, and the political economy of mass media. It also engages with the Hindi public sphere and politicians' use of social media platforms.

background_concepts.why_needed: Blogosphere and Twitter polarization studies quantify partisan clustering using graph-theoretic measures like modularity and assortative mixing, which are used as the analytic backbone but not derived or explained in the papers.

suggested_resources.title: Network Analysis and Modeling, CSCI 5352 Lecture 5 (Aaron Clauset, assortativity & modularity)
Social Network Analysis: Homophily (Donglei Du lecture notes)

name: Community networks and grassroots connectivity infrastructure
description: This domain addresses grassroots and community-owned connectivity solutions, including networks powered by community currencies and assisted digital distribution platforms for underserved populations. Readings emphasize participatory research with global community networks and the sustainability role played by women in rural networks. It also considers connectivity perspectives among small non-profit organizations serving marginalized groups like street children in Bangladesh.

background_concepts.why_needed: The community-currency-powered network reading proposes local currencies as a sustainability model without explaining what a complementary/community currency is or how mutual-credit and local exchange systems function.

suggested_resources.title: What complementary currencies look like (Mediterranean Co-Production Lab lesson)

background_concepts.concept: Wireless mesh networks and last-mile backhaul
background_concepts.why_needed: Grassroots connectivity readings assume familiarity with how community networks are physically built—mesh nodes, gateways, backhaul, and multi-hop routing—which underpins the sustainability and maintenance discussions but is not taught.

suggested_resources.title: Over The Air: Community Wireless and Mesh Networking Primer (Tech Learning Collective)

name: WhatsApp and social media adoption in the Global South
description: This domain explores how platforms like WhatsApp and Facebook are adopted and used in urban and rural India, and the broader Global South, examining sentiment, empowerment, and the social meaning of ICTs in context. Readings also probe the darker dynamics of social media, including fake news in developing countries and the filter bubble effects that shape information exposure. Together they map the mobile media actor-network and patterns of technology usage.

background_concepts.why_needed: The mobile media actor-network reading frames adoption through actor-network theory, treating human and non-human actors symmetrically, but assumes the reader knows this sociological framework rather than introducing it.

suggested_resources.title: The social meaning of ICTs / media theory context — Journalism University explainer on the public sphere and networked media

name: Gendered access to mobile phones and voice interfaces
description: This domain investigates how gender shapes access to and use of mobile phones and voice interfaces, including experimental work on whether the gender and accent of an IVR voice matter. Readings examine women livestock keepers using mobile phones in Zimbabwe and how personal characteristics and intermediaries influence girls' mobile appropriation in Nairobi. It highlights gendered barriers and mediation in mobile and mobile internet access.

name: Women-led participatory media initiatives
description: This domain centers on media platforms designed and run by women, drawing on experiences from a participatory media platform for and led by women in rural North India. It also features Khabar Lahariya, a rural women-run news network, illustrating how women lead grassroots journalism and content production. Together the readings foreground women's agency in participatory media.

name: AI-assisted content moderation and monitoring for climate/environmental discourse
description: This domain examines AI-based approaches to content moderation and media monitoring, particularly for environmental and climate discourse, including few-shot conservation media monitoring in low-resource languages and two-stage frameworks for climate content categorization. Readings also analyze moderation complexities in voice-based participatory media, AI risk coverage in news media, and community-centric design of text-to-image AI tools. It bridges technical moderation methods with their social and environmental implications.

background_concepts.why_needed: The environmental conservation media monitoring work centers on few-shot learning to classify content in low-resource languages, assuming the reader understands N-way-K-shot setups and why limited labeled data motivates this approach.

name: Media and information access for displaced and disabled populations
description: This domain addresses how marginalized populations, including visually impaired users, access media and information technologies, such as through case studies on IVR system use. Readings also examine Rohingya refugees' quest for democratic participation in the digital age and the limits of design in ensuring responsible technological outcomes for vulnerable groups. It emphasizes equity and responsible design for displaced and disabled communities.
